content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission UPERSEUED
May 24,
1993
Heidy Foster
Legislative Aide
Senator Dan McCorquodale
State Capitol,
Room 4032
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93--156
Dear Ms.
Foster:
You have requested advice on behalf of yourself and your
fiance, Peter Kellison,
concerning the lobbyist provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).’!
QUESTION
You are marrying a registered lobbyist.
He will pay for a
significant portion of the wedding reception.
Are the benefits
that will be provided to legislative officials at your wedding
reception subject to the $10 lobbyist gift prohibition and
reporting requirements?
CONCLUSION
Lobbyists are prohibited from making or arranging for the
making of gifts of more than $10 in a calendar month to
legislative officials and certain state agency officials.
In
addition,
lobbyists are required to disclose all gifts made to
such officials.
However,
Commission regulations provide an exception to the
definition of “gift” for hospitality (including food and
beverages)
provided by an individual in his or her home.
We
believe that food,
beverages and other nominal benefits provided
to an official at a wedding reception are very similar to benefits
provided in the context of home hospitality and should not be
limited or subject to diclosure.
This exception is applicable
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


Heidy Foster
Our File No.
A—93-156
Page
2
only to those benefits which are provided to all guests attending
the wedding.
In addition,
home hospitality provided by a lobbyist
is subject to certain restrictions which are outlined in the
Analysis set forth below.
FACTS
You are a legislative aide in the office of Senator Dan
McCorquodale.
You, plan to be married this fall to a registered
lobbyist, Peter Kellison,
who is employed by the California
Pharmacists Association.
Mr. Kellison is paying for a significant
portion of the wedding reception,
and persons who attend the
reception will receive food and beverages which exceed $10 in
value.
ANALYSIS
Section 86203 prohibits lobbyists from making or arranging
for the making of gifts of more than $10 in a calendar month to
any legislative official and to officials of state agencies the
lobbyist attempts to influence.
In addition,
lobbyists are
required to file periodic reports disclosing gifts made to those
officials.
(Section 86113.)
The term “gift” is defined in Section 82028 as a payment for
which consideration of equal or greater value has not been
received by the donor.
The term “payment” includes the rendering
of money,
property,
services or anything else of value, whether
tangible or intangible.
(Section 82044.)
Therefore,
food and
beverages provided to legislative and state agency officials are
gifts under the Act.
However,
in Regulation 18942, the Commission has provided an
exception to the definition of “gift” for “hospitality (including
food,
beverages,
or occasional lodging)
provided by an individual
in his or her home when the individual or a member of the
individual’s family is present, to an official.”
(Regulation
18942(a)(7)..)
This exception is also applicable to home
hospitality provided by a lobbyist, but is subject to certain
restrictions:
(a)
The cost of providing hospitality involving
food,
beverage or occasional lodging at the home of a
lobbyist is a gift within the meaning of Section 86203
and is reportable under the provisions of Section 86113
only if:
(1)
Any part of the cost of such hospitality is
paid for by the lobbyist’s employer or lobbying firm
directly;
or
(2)
The lobbyist is reimbursed by his or her
lobbyist employer or lobbying firm for any part of the
cost of such hospitality;
or


Heidy Foster
Our File No.
A-93-156
Page
3
(3)
The lobbyist deducts any part of the cost of
such hospitality as a business expense on any tax
return, either State or Federal; or
(4)
There is an understanding between the lobbyist
and his or her lobbyist employer or lobbying firm that
the amount of compensation received by the lobbyist
includes a portion to be utilized by the lobbyist to
provide gifts of hospitality in the lobbyist’s home.
(b)
In determining the applicability of
subsections
(a) (1)
through
(a) (4)
above,
the cost of
providing hospitality does not include any part of the
value of rental of the home of the lobbyist,
nor does it
include any depreciation on the premises where the
hospitality is extended.
Regulation 18630.
Although we realize wedding receptions are not ordinarily
held in the home of the bride or groom,
we feel that the personal
nature of a wedding and/or wedding reception is very similar to
entertainment provided in the home.
Therefore, the rationale
which applies to home entertainment should be similarly applied to
a wedding/wedding reception.
To the extent that none of the
activities outlined in Regulation 18630(a)
occur and officials who
attend your wedding reception do not receive benefits beyond those
provided to other guests,
food,
beverages and other nominal items
provided at the reception are not subject to the lobbyist gift
limit or to disclosure.
3
!
If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
This advice differs from that provided in the Dunmover Advice
Letter,
No. A-9l-459, which states that officials who attended a
lobbyist’s wedding reception must be disclosed on the lobbyist’s
disclosure report.
The Dunmoyer Advice Letter is rescinded.

6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Heidy Foster

A—93-156

the wedding.

is subject to certain restrictions which are outlined in the
Analysis set forth below.
